title: TPU v1 · Inside One MAC: INT8 Multiply with INT32 Accumulation
oneLine: Each systolic-array MAC multiplies 8-bit integers and accumulates in 32-bit integer precision. Low-precision multiplication saves area and power, high-precision accumulation prevents overflow across long dot products, and quantization or requantization occurs only at array boundaries—the key arithmetic and numeric-format trade-off.

y="388">Note: v1 discloses 8-bit multiply, 32-bit accumulation, and 4 MiB accumulator; per-MAC details are not public.</text></svg>

legend.name: Activation input a (INT8)
legend.desc: An 8-bit integer activation streams from Unified Buffer each cycle. Low bit width proportionally shrinks buses, registers, and multipliers across all 6.5×10⁴ array cells.

legend.name: INT8×INT8 multiplier
legend.desc: The cell’s only multiply takes two 8-bit operands and produces at most a 16-bit product. Multiplier area grows roughly with the square of width, so an 8-bit unit is dramatically smaller than a 32-bit floating-point multiplier—the central motivation for INT8.

legend.name: INT32 adder
legend.desc: Adds the 16-bit product into a running 32-bit partial sum. The adder uses higher precision: its cost grows roughly linearly with width while providing substantial dynamic-range headroom.

legend.name: INT32 accumulator register
legend.desc: Stores the 32-bit partial sum flowing down a column. It protects a 256-element dot product and repeated K-tile accumulation from overflow; high-precision accumulation is the numeric-safety margin.

legend.name: Quantization / requantization boundary
legend.desc: Precision conversion occurs only at array boundaries: activations and weights enter as INT8, while the outgoing INT32 result is scaled and clamped back to INT8 for the next layer. The array interior remains fixed-point.

steps.cap: ① An 8-bit activation a and resident 8-bit weight w enter the multiplier and produce a product of at most 16 bits. Low-precision multiplication deliberately saves area and power.
② The 16-bit product enters a 32-bit adder for accumulation into the running partial sum. Adder width exceeds product width to preserve headroom for long reductions.
③ The sum returns to the INT32 accumulator register and feeds back on the next cycle. Thousands of products can accumulate over time while 32-bit precision prevents overflow.
④ When the dot product completes, the 32-bit result reaches the array boundary, where scaling and clamping convert it back to INT8 for the next layer. Precision conversion stays outside each MAC.

before: Choose multiply and accumulation precision separately: This is the key decision. Inference tolerates low-precision weights and activations, so multiplication uses 8-bit integers; a dot product may add hundreds to hundreds of thousands of terms, so accumulation needs greater width to avoid overflow.

Calculate dynamic range and overflow bounds: An 8b×8b product requires at most 16 bits. Summing a 256-element column adds roughly log2(256)=8 guard bits, with further headroom for repeated K-tile passes. Compute the worst case before selecting accumulator width.

Specify the quantization scheme: Determine zero-point use, symmetric versus asymmetric quantization, per-tensor versus per-channel scale, and how scaling folds into boundary requantization. These choices set boundary circuitry and numeric error.

Known implementation facts: Public TPU v1 facts are a 256×256 array of 8-bit MACs, a 4 MiB 32-bit accumulator, and roughly 92 TOPS for INT8. Details such as carry-save structure and internal pipeline depth are not disclosed and follow common array practice here.
during: Multiply width: the 8-bit sweet spot: Multiplier area grows roughly with width squared, and a 6.5×10⁴-cell array is highly sensitive to each multiplier’s size. 8-bit balances acceptable inference accuracy against area and power; 4-bit raises accuracy risk, while 16-bit multiplies cost several times more; 8-bit is TPU v1’s choice.

Accumulator width: buy safety with linear cost: Adder cost scales approximately linearly with width. Expanding accumulation from 16 to 32 bits is inexpensive relative to the multiplier, while 32 bits provides ample margin beyond a 16-bit product plus worst-case carry growth, removing overflow as a design risk.

Push precision conversion to the boundary: Quantizing inside every cell would replicate scaling circuitry 6.5×10⁴ times and inject repeated rounding error. Keep the array fixed-point end to end—INT8 inputs and INT32 accumulation—and convert once at the input/output boundary.

Support clear and preload: Repeated K tiles contributing to one output must preserve accumulator state, while a new output tile must clear or preload it with bias. Provide clear/preload controls aligned with the controller FSM’s tile loop.

Place requantization and activation carefully: At the boundary, multiply the INT32 result by a scale, add bias, apply activation, and clamp to INT8. Power-of-2 scales can replace multiplication with shifts, reducing boundary area.

Evidence boundary: rounding and saturation details are not public: TPU v1 does not disclose whether conversion truncates or rounds, nor whether overflow saturates or wraps. Saturating output is common INT8 inference-ASIC practice because it avoids extreme-value wraparound; it is not a disclosed v1 choice.
after: Verify numerical correctness: Compare fixed-point outputs element by element against a reference that dequantizes before floating-point compute. Confirm INT8 multiply + INT32 accumulation + boundary requantization stays within tolerance and no path overflows 32 bits.

Run end-to-end accuracy evaluation: Deploy the quantization scheme across the full network and measure accuracy loss versus FP32. Confirm that 8-bit multiplication stays within the accuracy budget; for sensitive layers, feed results back to the model team for per-channel scaling or protected precision.
Stress overflow and boundaries: Use maximum-magnitude inputs and the longest K accumulation chain to validate 32-bit headroom and the intended boundary saturation or truncation behavior.

Hand off to later stages: Freeze multiply width, accumulator width, and quantization/requantization placement for synthesizable fixed-point RTL (⑤), and expose scale and bias configuration to the TensorFlow/XLA quantization software.

Document the evidence boundary: Distinguish published TPU v1 facts—8-bit multiply, 32-bit accumulation, 4 MiB accumulator, and 92 TOPS—from inferred implementation details such as rounding, saturation, and per-cell pipelining.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (8-bit multiply, 32-bit accumulation, the 4 MiB accumulator, and ~92 TOPS are public facts; per-cell MAC structure and rounding/saturation policy are completed from common INT8 inference-array practice)
